claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child (or children).**

**How old is the child you're claiming for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, let's start with one child first, and then we can check eligibility for the others.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14. Should I tell you about both of them now, or do you want to go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're welcome! It's great that you've told me about both children upfront - that's really helpful!

Since both **Alex (4 years old)** and **Blake (14 years old)** are under 16, they both automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. This is a good start!

Let me now move forward with the next check, which will apply to both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children are working:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14, I would expect neither of them is working 24+ hours per week - but please confirm this for me!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them works at all. Alex is only 4, so obviously not working, and Blake doesn't have a job either - just focuses on school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since neither Alex nor Blake is working, they both pass this check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by the child themselves (not benefits you receive for them).

Given their ages, it's very unlikely they would be receiving these, but I just need to confirm - are Alex and Blake free from receiving any such benefits in their own names?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. I receive benefits for them, but they don't claim anything themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's exactly right - benefits you receive for them are completely fine. It's only if the children themselves were claiming benefits in their own name that it would affect eligibility.

Both Alex and Blake pass this check too. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently on an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14 and focused on school, I'd expect the answer is no - but I just need to confirm that neither child is in an apprenticeship programme.

(For reference: most apprenticeships would disqualify a child from Child Benefit, except for Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are still approved.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them is doing an apprenticeship. Alex is way too young, and Blake is still in school - no apprenticeship for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Exactly as I expected! With Alex being 4 and Blake still in school, neither is in an apprenticeship.

Both children pass this check. Now we need to look at your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and your family
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

**Do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most common way people qualify)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm a resident here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You qualify as responsible if you meet **ANY ONE** of these:

1. **Living with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

**Do Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't - Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that important detail. Since the children have different living situations, their eligibility may differ.

Let me ask a follow-up question about Alex:

**Even though Alex doesn't live with you, do you contribute to Alex's upkeep?**

This could include:
- Paying maintenance under a court order or agreement
- Contributing money (weekly, monthly, or lump sums) towards Alex's care
- Paying for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents

The contribution needs to be at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind).

**Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep in any of these ways?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help out with things like clothes, food, and other expenses for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Just to confirm:

**Would you say your contributions towards Alex's care (clothes, food, and other expenses) amount to at least the same value as the Child Benefit payment?**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For reference, Child Benefit is paid weekly, and your contributions should be at least equivalent to that amount.

**Do your contributions to Alex's care (clothes, food, other expenses) add up to at least this level of support?** It doesn't have to be exactly weekly - it can be monthly or even lump sum payments throughout the year, as long as the overall contribution is equivalent.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's expenses, so that should be more than enough.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Yes, £50 per week is definitely more than enough to meet the contribution requirement for responsibility.

So to summarise so far:
- **Blake** - lives with you ✓
- **Alex** - you contribute to their upkeep ✓

You're considered responsible for both children. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question, especially given Alex's situation:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

Since Alex lives with their other parent, it's possible they might already be claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

- **For Blake** - Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
- **For Alex** - Is Alex's other parent (or anyone else) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No one else is claiming for Blake - he lives with me and I'm the only one claiming for him. 

But for Alex, yes, their other parent already claims Child Benefit for them since Alex lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being clear about that. This is an important distinction:

**For Blake:** No problem - you're the only one claiming, so we can proceed. ✓

**For Alex:** Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, this creates a situation where there would be two claimants for the same child. 

**Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any one time.** Generally, the person the child lives with has priority for the claim.

So for Alex, there are a few options:
1. **The other parent continues to claim** - Since Alex lives with them, they have priority
2. **You both agree to transfer the claim to you** - The other parent would need to agree to stop their claim so you could claim instead
3. **There's a dispute** - If you both want to claim and can't agree, HMRC would need to decide

**Has Alex's other parent agreed to transfer the Child Benefit claim for Alex to you, or will they continue to claim?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they haven't agreed to transfer it. They're continuing to claim Child Benefit for Alex since Alex lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes sense. Since Alex lives with their other parent and they are continuing to claim Child Benefit for Alex, they have priority for that claim.

Let me continue the assessment for **Blake**, as he is the child you can claim Child Benefit for.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Blake's current living situation:

**Where does Blake currently live?**

- **Normal living situation** - Blake lives at home with you in a regular home environment
- **In hospital** - Blake is currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Blake is currently in local authority care
- **Fostered** - Blake is in a fostering arrangement

Which of these best describes Blake's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives at home with me in a normal living situation - nothing special like hospital or care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, just one final check for Blake:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, Blake is supported by you (and/or other family members) rather than receiving financial support from the local council for his accommodation or upkeep.

Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. The council isn't paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance - I support him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Let me now provide you with the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility - Final Outcome

### ✅ Blake (14 years old) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake. He meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working 24+ hours per week
- Not receiving benefits in his own right
- Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- Blake lives with you
- No one else is claiming for him
- The local council isn't paying for his accommodation/maintenance

### ❌ Alex (4 years old) - **NOT ELIGIBLE (for you to claim)**

You **cannot claim** Child Benefit for Alex because their other parent is already claiming it. Since Alex lives with their other parent, that parent has priority for the claim. Only one person can receive Child Benefit per child at any one time.

---

## Next Steps for Blake's Claim

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Blake
3. **Provide Blake's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. Payments are made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

⚠️ **Important:** If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply. You can still claim, but you may have to pay some or all of it back through your tax return.

For more information or to apply, visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Blake meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) He is under 16 years old (14), (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right, (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements as a UK resident, (6) You are responsible for Blake as he lives with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) He lives in a normal home environment with you, and (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since Alex lives with their other parent, that parent has priority for the claim. The other parent has not agreed to transfer the claim to you, so they will continue to receive the benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with you and meets all the eligibility criteria. However, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex because Alex's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and since Alex lives with them, they have priority. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child at any one time.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Blake meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) He is under 16 years old (14), (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right, (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements as a UK resident, (6) You are responsible for Blake as he lives with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) He lives in a normal home environment with you, and (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since Alex lives with their other parent, that parent has priority for the claim. The other parent has not agreed to transfer the claim to you, so they will continue to receive the benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with you and meets all the eligibility criteria. However, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex because Alex's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and since Alex lives with them, they have priority. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child at any one time.
